DKey.introduction: This key is based on the work of Gauld and Bolton (1988). Gauld, I., Bolton, B. (1988). The Hymenoptera. British Museum (Natural History), London. The key was prepared using DKey software (Tofilski 2018). Tofilski A., 2018. DKey software for editing and browsing dichotomous keys. ZooKeys 735: 131-140. [<u>https://doi.org/10.3897/zookeys.735.21412</u>](https://doi.org/10.3897/zookeys.735.21412)

lead.text: Body without a marked constriction between the 1st and 2nd abdominal segments (Fig. 58, 64). Wings: fully developed, fore wing: with an enclosed anal cell (Fig. 16, 59, 61). [Symphyta]

Body with a constriction between the 1st and 2nd abdominal segments (Fig. 121), if the constrictipn is secondarily obscured (Fig. 96) than fore wing without enclosed cells. Wings: fully developed, vestigal or absent, fore wing: if present then without an enclosed anal cell (Fig. 17, 76, 115). [Apocrita]
Antenna: inserted on ventral side of head, close to mandibles (Fig. 40). Anal cell of fore wing: indistinctly delineated.
Antenna: inserted on anterior side of head, away from mandibles. Anal cell of fore wing: distinct.
Vein RS of fore wing: branched (Fig. 57). Antenna: with third segment long and stout, followed by a filament of 9 or more slender segments (Fig. 41).
Vein RS of fore wing: unbranched. Antenna: different.
Fore tibia: with one apical spur, if the second spur is present it is much shorter or vestigal.
Fore tibia: with two well-developed apical spurs, both apical spurs are of similar size.
Cenchri: absent (Fig. 42). Abdomen: terminally laterally compressed, anteriorly slightly constricted between first and second segments (Fig. 74).
Cenchri: present (Fig. 43). Abdomen: cylindrical or depressed, not constricted anteriorly (Fig. 72, 73).

Pronotum: in dorsal view with hind margin more or less straight (Fig. 44). Vein 2r-rs of fore wing: present. Labrum: concealed (Fig. 46). Mid tibiae and hind tibiae: with preapical spurs. Antenna with 18 or more segments (Fig. 58).

Pronotum: in dorsal view with hind margin strongly bowed (Fig. 45, or if weakly bowed then fore wing with 2r-rs absent. Vein 2r-rs of fore wing: present or absent. Labrum: exposed (Fig. 47). Mid tibiae and hind tibiae: without preapical spurs, if preapical spurs present, then antenna with fewer than 10 segments. Antenna: with 3-32 segments, often with 9 or less.
Segment 1 of gaster forming a node or scale, or first two segments nodiform, so segment 2 is deeply separated (both dorsally and ventrally) from segments 1 and 3 (Fig. 143).
Segment 1 of gaster not scale-like, if rarely slightly nodiform then with segment 2 closely adapted to segment 3.

Segment 1 of gaster inserted high up on propodeum so gap between propodeal foramen and insertion of hind coxa is about equal to or greater than gap between foramen and hind margin of metanotum (Fig. 79-80).
Segment 1 of gaster inserted low down on propodeum so gap between propodeal foramen and insertion of hind coxa is very much less than gap between foramen and hind margin of metanotum.
Antenna: with 14 or fewer segments. Fore wing with costal cell distinct (Fig. 79, 80).
Antenna: with 18 or more segments. Fore wing with costal cell obliterated, veins C, Sc, R and Rs fused between wing base and pterostigma (Fig. 120)
Wings: fully developed.
Wings: vestigal or absent.
Fore wing with one enclosed cell, or without any enclosed cells (Fig. 98, 99, 100, 108; 111, 112, 113; 132)
Fore wing with two or more cells clearly delineated by veins (Fig. 14, 15, 81, 110, 119, 120, 121, 122, 144).
Fore wing with membrane reticulate; hind wing vestigial, with a bifid apex; segments 1 and 2 of gaster cylindrical, slender, forming a 2-segmented petiole (Fig. 48) (Body length less than 1 mm).

Fore wing membrane not reticulate; hind wing fully developed though often very narrow, but never with a bifid apex; gaster with at most first segment cylindrical and slender so petiole, if present, is 1-segmented.
Hind wing with a distinct stalk (Fig. 49).
Hind wing not stalked.
Alitrunk with pronotum not extending back to tegulae (Fig. 50); wings without enclosed cells.
Alitrunk with pronotum extending back to almost touch tegulae (Fig. 51); wings with or without closed cells.
Antennae inserted in centre of face, their sockets separated from the clypeus by more than twice their own diameter.
Antennae inserted on face close to clypeus, their sockets separated from clypeus by about their own diameter or less.
Head distinctly prognathous; tergite 1 of gaster as long as following tergites, separated from sternite, posteriorly overlapping tergite 2 (Fig. 135)

Head hypognathous; tergite 1 of gaster shorter than the following apparent tergite (which may be a syntergite), or the two fused and tergite 1 visible as a ridged anterior rim of the first apparent tergite; tergite 1 usually fused with sternite to form a ring at anterior end of gaster.
Fore wing with vein along anterior margin, this vein distally with a curved stigmal branch, sometimes with a large pterostigma (Fig. 53, 113); fore tibia with two spurs

Fore wing without any venation, or with a short vein that does not reach to level of middle of wing, or if with a long vein, then this is proximally separated from anterior margin of wing, and its stigmal branch is almost straight; pterostigma not present (Fig. 112); fore tibia with a single spur.

First tergite of gaster short, fused with sternite to form a ring-like, highly sclerotized segment; second tegite (or syntergite) longer than tergites 1 and 3+ combined (Fig. 52); spiracles not present at least on first and apparent second gastral tergites; fore wing usually with 2 enclosed cells (costal and radial)(Fig. 110), rarely with up to 3 more enclosed cells (thus making a maximum of 5)(Fig. 109)

First tergite of gaster quite long, not fused with sternite to form short ring-like segment; second tergite not longer than tergites 1 and 3+ combined; first and second segments of gaster with distinct spiracles (though these may be positioned ventrally on laterotergite); fore wing usually with 6 or more enclosed cells, rarely with 2-5 and then always with basal and subbasal cells enclosed, costal cell usually enclosed, radial cell sometimes not enclosed.
Fore wing with radial cell either not indicated or open distally, and without any complete submarginal (cubital) cells; hind wing without enclosed cells (Fig. 136, 137, 138).
Fore wing with an enclosed radial cell, and with at least one enclosed cubital cell; hind wing generally with two or three enclosed cells.
Pronotum with upper hind corner widely separated from tegulae, and lower down the side with a pronounced prontal lobe covering mesothoracic spiracle (Fig. 55).
Pronotum with upper hind corner reaching to or close to tegula, with or without a pronounced prontal lobe (Fig. 56).
Antennae with 16 or more segments, more or less filiform, unspecialized; sternites of gaster weakly sclerotized, tending to dry with median longitudinal fold
Antenna with 15 or fewer segments, sometimes filiform and unspecialized, often geniculate with elongate scape and clavate distal segments; sternites of gaster strongly sclerotized .
Fore, mid and hind tarsi 3-segmented.
All tarsi with 4 or 5 segments.
Upper hind corner of pronotum separated from tegula by a prepectus
Upper hind corner of prontum more or less touching tegula, or with tegula absent
First segment of gaster more or less conical, not dorsally fused with tegite 2; tergites 1 and 2 with spiracles.
First segment of gaster cylindrical or annular, or minute, indistinct, fused dorsally with tergite 2; tergites 1 and 2 without spiracles.
Antenna with 10 segments.
Antenna with 11-13 segments.
Head prognathous and dorsoventrally flattened; clypeus with a median carina extending between antennae.
Head hypognathous, not dorsoventrally flattened; clypeus lacking a median carina that extends between antennae.
Antenna never geniculate, the scape only slightly longer than broad, and slightly shorter than first flagellar segment; gaster laterally compressed

Female (the most commonly encountered brachypterous or apterous sex) with antenna geniculate, the scape elongate, at least twice the length of the first flagellar segment; gaster of both sexes cylindrical or depressed.
Anterior tibia with two apical spurs.
Anterior tibia with one apical spur.
Antennal socket separated from clypeus by its own diameter or less; first segment of gaster with tergite and sternite separate; tergite 8 without spiracle; antenna with 12 or fewer segments.

Antennal socket separated from clypeus by more than twice its own diameter; first segment of gaster with tergite and sternite fused; tergite 8 with spiracle; antenna almost always with 13 or more segments.

Gaster, especially in females, laterally compressed; antenna of female 14-segmented, of male 13-segmented; ovipositor concealed; head without shelf-like process; cerci absent; ovipositor opening ventral.

Gaster usually cylindrical; antennae with various numbers of segments, if 14-segmented in female or 13-segmented in male then ovipositor exposed or antenna mounted on a shelf-like process of the face; cerci present; ovipositor opening terminal

term.definition: Fig. 15. Ichneumonidae - fore wing. (CC0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 16. Xyelidae - fore wing. (Joanna Jurkowska, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 17. Apidae - wings. (Gabriela Karaszkiewicz, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 40. Orussus - head, anterior view. (Adam Tofilski, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 41. Xyela - antenna. (Adam Tofilski, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 42. Cephus - abdomen, dorsal view. (Adam Tofilski, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 43. Sirex - abdomen, dorsal view. (Adam Tofilski, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 44. Megalodontes - pronotum, dorsal view. (Julia Zawada, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 45. Tenthredo - pronotum, dorsal view. (Kinga Ślęczek, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 46. Megalodontes - head, anterior view. (Maja Tomaszewska, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 47. Tenthredo - head, anterior view. (Weronika Stelnicka, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 48. Palaeomymar (Mymarommatidae) - petiole and gaster, lateral view. (Zofia Kościuszko, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 51. Proctotrupes - anterior part of mesosoma, lateral view. (Aleksandra Kwiecień, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 52. Proctotrupes - metasoma, lateral view. (Maria Komorowska, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 55. Ectemnius (Sphecidae) - anterior part of mesosoma, lateral view. (Emilia Karaś, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 58. *Pamphilius sylvaticus* (Pamphiliidae). (Maria Komorowska, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 59. Blasticotoma - fore wing. (Zofia Kościuszko, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 60. Cimbex - antenna. (Adam Tofilski, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 61. Tenthredo - fore wing. (Dagmara Kowalczyk, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 64. *Arge pagana* (Argidae) - fore wing. (CC0, from Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 65. Diprion pini (Diprionidae) - fore wing. (Liszka Joanna, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 66. Tenthredo arcuata (Tenthredinidae) - fore wing. (Agnieszka Łatka, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 72. Urocerus gigas (Siricidae). (CC0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 73. Xiphydria (Xiphydriidae) - fore wing. (Natalia Malinowska, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 75. Trigonalis hahnii (Trigonalyidae) - wing. (Tomasz Orkisz, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 76. Aulacus - fore wing. (Wiktoria Pachel, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 77. Evania - fore wing. (Jakub Pacyga, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 78. Aulacus - head. (Adam Tofilski, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 79. Brachygaster minuta (Evaniidae) - wings. (Sara Prystupa, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 80. Gasteruptrion jucator (Gasteruptiidae) - fore wing. (Weronika Stelnicka, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 82. Figites - fore wing. (Tymoteusz Tuttas, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 83. Andricus (Cynipidae) - fore wing. (Karolina Szmyracha, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 84. Anacharis eucharioides (Figitidae) - fore wing. (Kinga Ślęczek, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 85. Tribliographa rapae (Eucoilidae) - fore wing. (Maja Tomaszewska, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 88. Arrhenophagus (Encyrtidae) - fore wing. (Dagmara Kowalczyk, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 89. Arrhenophagus - head, anterior facial view. (Karolina Szmyracha, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 92. Torymus - fore wing. (Adam Tofilski, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 96. *Thysanus ater* (Signiphoridae), female - dorsal view.
Fig. 114. Banchus (Ichneumonidae) - hind wing. (Adam Tofilski, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 115. Banchus (Ichneumonidae) - fore wing. (Jakub Więckowski, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 117. Macrocentrus - fore wing. (Julia Zawada, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 120. Amblyteles armatorius (Ichneumonidae) - fore wing. (Tymoteusz Tuttas, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
Fig. 121. *Meteorus ictericus* (Braconidae), female - dorsal view.
Fig. 124. Dolichurus (Sphecidae) - fore wing. (Adam Tofilski, CC BY-NC 3.0, after Gauld and Bolton 1988)
